tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • unknown: 3 occurrences<>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • IDIV: 1 occurrences<>
  • VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 1 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.workaround: Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 29.25 cycles. At this rate:
  • 11% of peak load performance is reached (7.38 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 16% of peak store performance is reached (5.20 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 24.00 cycles (1.22x speedup).

header:
Warnings:
  • The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
  • Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions. Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
1% of peak computational performance is used (0.17 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 9.25 cycles (3.16x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
22% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 15% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 13% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 37% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 32% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 21.94 cycles (1.33x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.title: Expensive FP math instructions/calls
potential.txt:
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 27.00 cycles (1.08x speedup).

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
  • unknown: 3 occurrences<>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • IDIV: 1 occurrences<>
  • VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 32, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 32 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 32) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>

header:
Warnings:
  • The number of fused uops of the instruction [ENDBR64] is unknown
  • Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions. Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
1% of peak computational performance is used (0.17 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 9.25 cycles (3.16x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:
    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
22% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 15% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 13% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 37% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 32% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 21.94 cycles (1.33x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.title: Expensive FP math instructions/calls
potential.txt:
Detected performance impact from expensive FP math instructions/calls.
By removing/reexpressing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 29.25 to 27.00 cycles (1.08x speedup).
